Josh’s comments on HC change…interesting
stories.summary:
An article I just read quotes Josh as follows:



	 



	“We’ve obviously got a new head coach, and so to have those memories being made in a new spot,” Allen said. “I think change is needed sometimes, especially when we’ve been close in the past, even going back to 20-ish, 30-ish years ago, just how close the Bills have been to winning a Super Bowl — I think this is kind of a switch that was necessary and I’m very excited.”



	 



	Wow. This is the first time I’m aware of that Josh has been this definitive (publicly) on the topic.



	 



	I’m really expecting some exciting and good things this year.

stories.title: Terrel Bernard's "Horrible Contract Extension"
stories.summary:
I'm sure many of you listen to Locked On Bills. This week, Joe was lamenting how the Bills are stuck with Terrel Bernard's contract and it's one of Beane's worst.



	 



	Someone please correct me if I'm wrong, but this is how I read his contract:



	 



	2026: $5.35M Cap Hit



	2027: $11.15M Cap Hit



	2028: $12M Cap Hit, but he can be released and save $5.5M 



	 



	Yes, I understand he's often injured. However, he flashed more playmaking potential than Edmunds ($18M/yr with the Bears) ever did.



	Obviously respected in the locker room to be 1 of 2 team captains and he just turned 27 years old.



	$5M this year, $11M next year, and a $6.5M dead cap hit the following year if we want to cut him isn't that bad.



	 



	I'm sick of hearing this is a terrible contract we can't get away from.

T McGee’s 2026 NHL Draft Post-Mortem & Peek at 2027
stories.summary:
DBTB –



Well, as I have said many times before, let no one claim that I am an unaccountable draftnik who throws out his rankings and then runs for cover, never to speak about it again!  I’ve always wanted to have the meteorologists (aka weathermen/ladies) get on TV and announce their accuracy percentages, although that will never happen.  But given my situation here at DBTB, I wanted to walk the walk and talk the talk.  Or insert favorite cliché here.  So here it is, my accuracy metrics for 2026!  



This was a tough year for me.  The success of the Sabres had me more focused on the Blue and Gold than I had been in a while.  That left me less time to focus on the Draft than normal, and that’s reflected in my relatively poor performance as far as predicting things.  Granted, it wasn’t SO far off previous years, but it was off, for the most part.  So mea culpa, mea culpa!      



I know due to a lot of logistical snafus, you all didn’t get to see my final Mock Draft.  For the three of you that care.  But, just to be clear, I did finish my Mock Draft on the Monday before the Draft.  So I was unable to take into account some major events:  not the Brady Tkachuk deal, the Will Eklund trade, nor the Bowen Byram trade or the Pavel Dorofeyev deal.  Some moves that had a pretty big impact on how parts of the Draft went down, honestly. 



Not an excuse, but I would’ve done the Draft a bit differently had I been able to take those deals into account.  I might have gotten 2-3 more selections correct had those deals come down a few days earlier.  Let me emphasize, MIGHT. 



Here we go.  This is how I did for each round in terms of accuracy.  This is not exact slots, but rather a player that went off the Board in the Round in which I had him going.  So I did not have Ilia Morozov going at #20, but I did get him going in Round 1, so that’s one right for me.  Just to beclear about how the scorekeeping went.    



Round 1: 



I finished with 25 of 32, which is pretty low for me.  Usually, I’m up around 27-28 right out of 32 for the first round.  But the bunch in the late 1st through the mid 2nd made it tough to pick and choose.  As far as the Sabres go, I had Rudolph at #11 in my Mock Draft, and Morozov at #16.  I had 5 defenders go off the Board before I had Rudolph getting chosen.  Not a great start for me.



Round 2: 



I did about standard for the 2nd.  Got 11 of 31 (remember, Vegas had to forfeit their 2nd round draft pick due to some silliness with Tortorella and the media) right.  Will admit I was surprised by a couple of players that I had for sure going as high as the late 1st that didn’t go in the 2nd.  Again, a little below my normal number, but not far off. 



Round 3:



Things picked up a little here as I got 10 of 32.  Typically, the 3rd is when I start to fall off, but I stayed pretty close to the 2nd round, which doesn’t always happen.  And I just missed on a couple guys that went at the end of the 2nd, and a couple more that went at the beginning of the 4th.  Could have easily finished with half (16) right! 



Round 4: 



Speaking of the 4th, this is where things start to go off the rails.  And no different this year, as I only managed 7 of 32 in this round.  Interestingly, I had Sabres’ selection Olivier Murnieks going at #123 overall, just one spot off from where the Sabres actually chose him.  I was close on that one at least!



Round 5: 



A decent showing here with 6 of 32, which is especially gratifying since this was the first round of the Draft where multiple guys I didn’t have on my Board started appearing. Guys who I had either removed during the year or never even made it on to my radar got selected in the 5th round.  As for the Sabres, their 5th round selection – Doman Szongoth – I did not have getting chosen until the 7th round.  So a miss for me there! 



Round 6:



Predictably, I went miserable 3 for 32 in this round.  I was surprised to see a number of players that I had much higher go in the 6th. Unfortunately for me, the Sabres choice, Dylan Dumont, was not in my Top 200 and I can’t even remember watching him this season.  Supposedly, he grew a few inches during the year.  Drummondville, where Dumont played, had one goaltender that I caught a couple of times this year, and another late riser in Louis-Felix Bourque (who went in the 3rd round, where I had him going!).  Somehow I just flat-out missed Dumont. 



Round 7: 



Last but not least, I managed a meager 2 of 32 in the 7th round.  I did have Sabres’ development camp invite Cohen Bidgood going in the middle of the 7th round, so I imagine they would’ve taken him if they had a selection in the 7th.  Always the toughest round to predict, my rather sub-par year for 2026 culminated in a pretty bleak 7th round showing.  In 2025, I think I managed to get 5 or 6 right in the 7th round but really struggled in rounds 4-5-6.  So I guess it all balanced out in the end. 



What this should tell you all is that I barely know what I’m talking about!  But I want to thank Calvin, Melissa, Chad, Jedi, and all the DBTB brain trust for giving me a platform to post my draft and prospect blathering for another year.  Even a year when our beloved Sabres didn’t care much for the Draft, prospects, or anything other than beating the brakes off Boston and almost squashing Montreal. 



As always, feel free to ping me anytime with any questions or comments about the Draft, Sabre prospects, or my (putrid) performance as a prognosticator. 



As you know, the cycle that supplies the NHL with an ever-growing pot of talent never stops. So I’ll offer you a quick Watch List for next season, which looks to have some really strong forwards at the top of the Draft (unlike this past year, which really had the blueliners dominating), including a few potential #1 centermen…which always makes a Draft stand out. This is in no particular order, just my Top 32 at this moment in time: 



TOP 32 for 2027:



Landon Dupont, 6’0 RHD, WHL



Nazar Privalov, 6’4 RW, RUS



Milan Sundstrom, 6’3 C, SWE



Dima Zhilkin, 5’10 RW, OHL



Sammy Nelson, 6’2 C, US NTDP



Noah Davidson, 6’3 LW, WHL



Tim Kazda, 6’1 W, USHL



Vladimir Shtyrkhunov, 6’0 RHD, RUS



Alexis Joseph, 6’5 C, QMJHL



Carter Meyer, 6’1 C, US NTDP



Dorian Eklund Aspe, 6’4 C, SWE



Andrei Pustovoy, 6’3 RW, RUS



Oskars Lukks, 6’5 LHD, FIN



Oliver Ozgony, 6’3 LW, USHL



Yevgeni Yakunin, 6’3 C, RUS



Jonah Neuenschwander, 6’4 LW, SWISS



Shaeffer Gordon-Carroll, 6’0 LW, WHL



Kohyn Eshkawkogan, 6’0 RHD, OHL



Brock England, 5’11 C, WHL (McGee Favorite!)



Luca Santala, 6’2 C, FIN



Jaako Wycisk, 6’1 C, OHL



Sergei Skvortsov, 6’0 LW, RUS



Boston Tait, 6’2 LHD, WHL



Jaxon Jacobsen, 5’10 C, WHL



Petr Tomek, 5’11 C, CZE



Roberts Naudins, 6’6 LW, US HS



Roman Kolzhevnikov, 6’2 LW, RUS



Loui Karlsson, 5’10 W, SWE



Diego Guttierez, 6’2 RHD, US NTDP



Ryerson Edgar, 5’10 C, OHL



Lenni Kokkonen, 6’2 RHD, FIN



Adam Nemec, 6’0 LW, CZE

Brandin Cooks remains in contact with Bills and wants to come back
stories.summary:
Extensive story in The Athletic: https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/7425977/2026/07/09/nfl-bills-sean-mcdermott-brandin-cooks-catch/



	 



	‘He wants to return to the Bills, and the interest apparently is mutual. Bills football boss Brandon Beane said in March the team had stayed in contact with Cooks’ camp, and Cooks said he talked with new head coach Joe Brady recently.



	 



	Any contract will likely be team friendly. Cooks doesn’t need the money. He has earned $124.9 million over his career, less than just nine receivers all time. He flies his own Cirrus Vision Jet, although he’s in the market for a new one. The Oregon home where he lives with his college-sweetheart wife, Briannon, and their three children was featured in Architectural Digest two years ago.



	 



	But you can’t buy a Super Bowl ring.



	 



	“I know what teams don’t know right now,” Cooks’ agent, Ryan Tollner, said. “He’s working in the dark right now as hard as anyone you could imagine.



	 



	“He’s a master of controlling what he can control. Even the way the season ended, he was crushed because he wants to win, but I would guess that Brandin would rather have the opportunity to make a play and have the ball come to him. That’s what he works so relentlessly for.”



	Tollner said plenty of contenders have extended offers, that an exciting choice will be made, but Cooks has emphasized his wish for one team in particular. Cooks is done rewinding that replay, but he wants to run it back with Buffalo.



	 



	Training camp, Cooks said, is “the purest form of football,” so he intends to be on a roster within the next few weeks.



	 



	“Obviously, Buffalo is the place I’d love to be,” Cooks said. “I want to prove that to them and have a full offseason with them. Both sides are figuring things out. We’ll see, but hopefully something transpires because I love going to training camp. That’s where you build that callus.”‘

stories.title: Batavia Party Zone
stories.summary:
What say you all about us advocating for a corner of the new stadium to be officially (or unofficially, if we must) recognized as the “Batavia Party Zone”?



	We not too old people remember a time, before the league was whitewashed, when fans could actually tape signs on The Stadium Wall (no pun intended because it’s true) and one of those signs every home game was…”The Batavia Party Zone…” It was unmistakable and existed proudly during every game in OP for about 15-20 years. I’m feeling nostalgic tonight, is anyone else smelling what I’m cooking?

stories.title: Following career year, Sabres’ Peyton Krebs files for salary arbitration
stories.summary:
Buffalo Sabres forward Peyton Krebs has filed for salary arbitration, the NHL Players’ Association announced Sunday.
Krebs, 25, is the Sabres’ only unsigned restricted free agent.
Filing, of course, doesn’t mean a player will go to arbitration. Negotiations can continue. The sides usually reach a deal before the arbitration hearing.

Players who file for arbitration want more than their qualifying offer would pay them.
Fifteen players filed for arbitration, according to the NHLPA.
The deadline ... Read the full article 
The post Following career year, Sabres’ Peyton Krebs files for salary arbitration first appeared on Buffalo Hockey Beat.

stories.title: Sabres add goalie depth for Rochester by signing Matt Villalta
stories.summary:
The Buffalo Sabres on Saturday added goalie depth, signing Matt Villalta to a one-year, two-way contract.
Villalta, 27, gives the Sabres a veteran netminder for the Rochester Americans. He will likely share the net with prospects Topias Leinonen or Scott Ratzlaff, both of whom spent time in the AHL last season.
The 6-foot-3, 190-pound Villalta has spent most of his career in the AHL, compiling 123-83-20 record with a 2.93 goals-against average, a .903 save ... Read the full article 
The post Sabres add goalie depth for Rochester by signing Matt Villalta first appeared on Buffalo Hockey Beat.

stories.title: T McGee’s Quick NHL Draft Recap
stories.summary:
DBTB – 



I’m back from the Draft in scenic BFLO.  A few thoughts that popped into my head during the weekend: 



– If any of you ever get the chance to go to the Draft, you should go.  It is a life-affirming experience.  You’re sitting among the aspiring draftees, their families, friends and coaches, watching people have their dreams fulfilled right in front of you.  Really amazing to be a part of.  And really supportive.  Fans, some of whom have probably never heard of these kids, giving them high-fives and encouraging them.  Really cool.  



– Daxon Rudolph is a guy who I had on my Board at #9.  I think if he can get a little quicker and play with more consistent physicality (probably get stronger), he can be a really good #3.  I’ve watched some more of his shifts since they chose him and there’s one guy he reminds me of..,this will be weird…but I think Sergei Zubov when I watch him.  Everything seems so easy that he doesn’t always feel like he’s trying, he has this crazy knack of just getting pucks through crowds to the net, always moving but never chasing, really clever with manipulating defenders and sees the ice a step ahead. 



– Morozov I had at #21 on my Board, so he went right about where I expected.  I think what’s being missed about this kid are two things: 1) his work ethic is absolutely through the roof, and 2) when you’re the youngest kid in NCAA hockey, on a not-great team, you’re going to have to play conservatively if you want to stay on the ice.  He’s going to have better players next season, they will be a decent team.  I think he’s going to put up bigger numbers and show a lot more offensively in Year 2.  The discussion will change from “probably a 3C, maybe a really good 4C” to “maybe a 2C, a 3C on a really good team”.  He could be a bigger, more physical Anton Lundell. 



– Zellweger is really interesting.  I went back and looked at my 2021 Board, and he was way down at #73.  Probably because he was 5’9.  I don’t know if Ruff can get him to defend the way the coach wants, but he has tremendous wheels, a really good release, and loves to exploit seams in the D (the same way Byram did).  I think he starts as a 3rd pair, and they see how he does in the system.  Everyone went a little coo-coo with Byram’s 11 goals – Zelly had 7 goals and probably played about 5-6 minutes less/game.  Offensively, he won’t be a problem.  Can he get the puck out on time and with possession?  That will be the big question. 



– Murnieks I had at #90.  So great value in my mind for the pick.  The really intriguing thing is early in the year, he was a late 1st rounder.  He started off well, got banged up (I didn’t realize he had multiple concussions this season) and tailed off, and then had a good finish in the U-18s.  He’s a do-everything type of centerman who works his tail off and as a late birthday, will probably get bigger before all is said and done.  He’s probably 4 years away (next year in the Q, 2 years at Boston College, and then maybe a year in the AHL) but could wind up being a really good 4C.  There’s a lot of value in that. 



– Szongoth is an interesting player I had at #158, so kind of freaky that he went at #156.  He’s going to need a lot of time.  There’s some rawness to his game that he gets away with in his age group due to his size and strength.  But he flashes some nice touch offensively, and defensively he’s very methodical and reliable.  I imagine he stays in Finland until he gets a regular shift in Liiga.  But anyone with two first names (Domon Krystzof) who plays for a team named KooKoo is someone you have to take seriously. 



– I was a little disappointed that the Sabres did not add to the current roster at the Draft.  It was all the buzz on the Draft floor, that something big was coming, and it seems they got very close with Winnipeg.  But they didn’t panic and overpay.  Unlike Florida, or Vegas, or LA, the Sabres don’t have a choice but to be a primarily ‘draft and develop’ team.  The city is not a draw, the weather is not a draw, and for most players in their early 20s, the Sabres have been a bottom feeder for their entire life (until last season).  A high pick fell into their laps for Byram, and they used it.  It’s not the perfect outcome, but it’s a pretty smart one nonetheless.

stories.title: Prospect Prokhor Poltapov could be ‘Swiss Army knife’ for Sabres
stories.summary:
BUFFALO – After his contract in the Kontinental Hockey League expires next year, the Sabres expect prospect Prokhor Poltapov to come to North America and immediately jump into their lineup.
Where could the Russian, a winger the Sabres drafted in the second round in 2021, 33rd overall, fit in?
Tim Kennedy, the Sabres’ new director of player development, worked closely with Poltapov in his previous role as a development coach. He believes the versatile ... Read the full article

stories.summary:
When the Stanley Cup Playoffs began in April, Buffalo Sabres newcomer Olen Zellweger found himself watching in the press box as a healthy scratch after enjoying regular duty all season.
Naturally, Zellweger, a defenseman the Sabres acquired in a trade last Friday with the Anaheim Ducks after dealing Bowen Byram, initially felt frustrated. But he said getting benched taught him some lessons.
He started getting back to basics. From his seat high above the ice, ... Read the full article

stories.title: Sabres sign Aidan McDonough to two-way contract; Matthew Peca inks AHL deal with Rochester

Two established scoring forwards should be joining the Rochester Americans next season.
The Buffalo Sabres on Thursday signed Aidan McDonough to a one-year, two-way contract. Meanwhile, veteran Matthew Peca inked a two-year AHL deal.
McDonough, 26, played with the Wilkes-Barre/Scranton Penguins last season on an AHL contract, setting career highs across the board by registering 23 goals, a team-high 44 points, a plus-18 rating and 24 penalty minutes in 65 games.

He will receive $850,000 ... Read the full article

stories.title: Sabres make depth signings on Day One of Free Agency
stories.summary:
No big names to get the fan base excited so far



The Buffalo Sabres were pretty quiet yesterday as NHL Free Agency got underway at noon. 




We have signed forward Conor Sheary and defenseman Dennis Gilbert to one-way contracts worth $850,000. Additionally, we have signed forwards Trevor Kuntar (two years) and Jason Polin (one year) to two-way contracts.Details → https://t.co/PiyYceeGZC— Buffalo Sabres (@BuffaloSabres) July 1, 2026




Two familiar faces to the organization made a return as forward Conor Sheary and defenseman Dennis Gilbert are back, signing NHL veteran minimum $850,000 one-way contracts. 



The pair will have a chance to play for roster spots when training camp begins, but otherwise nothing to really get excited about. 



The Rochester Amerks cupboard is quite bare so the Sabres added Trevor Kuntar and Jason Polin for some veteran leadership. 



It’s hard to tell if there’s more coming, especially with General Manager Jarmo Kekäläinen sounding like he’s done wheeling and dealing. 




"If it’s a perfect fit and also, financially and cap-wise, it fits our system, then we’re open for business.”More quotes from general manager Jarmo Kekäläinen after the start of free agency → https://t.co/2laNxSHQ4l— Buffalo Sabres (@BuffaloSabres) July 1, 2026

stories.title: Sabres trade away Levi to the Oilers
stories.summary:
Buffalo gets a third round draft pick for the goalie prospect



Devon Levi has been traded away by the Buffalo Sabres to the Edmonton Oilers along with a seventh round pick in return for a 2028 third round draft pick. 



After a highly-promising start to the goaltender’s career, he seemed to have hit a rut this last season with the Amerks. 




We have acquired a third-round pick in the 2028 NHL Draft from the Edmonton Oilers in exchange for goaltender Devon Levi and a seventh-round pick in the 2028 NHL Draft.Details → https://t.co/Si8ZSPznST pic.twitter.com/qzKxylF0kw— Buffalo Sabres (@BuffaloSabres) July 1, 2026




Through 120 games as an Amerk, Levi posted 64 wins with 10 shutouts and a 2.52 GAA and .914 SV%.



With the Sabres, Levi had a 3.29 GAA and a .894 SV% in 39 games with 17 games.

BUFFALO – After free agency opened Wednesday, the Sabres stayed pretty quiet, signing four depth players likely ticketed for the minors and trading goalie Devon Levi.
While more notable moves could materialize – “We’re still open for business,” general manager Jarmo Kekalainen said in LECOM Harborcenter – the roster might be pretty much set.
“By no means we’re desperate to add anything right now,” Kekalainen said. “It needs to be a perfect fit both from ... Read the full article

stories.title: Sabres did ‘due diligence’ before hiring Milan Lucic for scouting job

BUFFALO – Before they hired former NHL winger Milan Lucic as a pro scout, general manager Jarmo Kekalainen said the Sabres did their “due diligence” on his arrest in 2023 on suspicion of assault and battery on a family member.
Prosecutors later dropped a domestic violence charge.
“We take all those things very seriously, but we have also seen and heard that he’s done a lot of personal growth,” Kekalainen said Wednesday in LECOM Harborcenter.... Read the full article

Sheary, ... Read the full article

BUFFALO – Goalie Devon Levi’s time with the Sabres organization has ended.
The Sabres this afternoon traded Levi, 24, and a seventh-round pick in 2028 to the Edmonton Oilers in exchange for a third-round pick in 2028.
The Oilers have struggled to find a No. 1 goalie, so Levi, who must clear waivers next season before going to the minors, should have a strong opportunity in Edmonton.
Levi, once a highly touted prospect, fell ... Read the full article

stories.title: Zellweger Signs With the Sabres
stories.summary:
New Defenseman Inks Three Year Contract With Buffalo



Former Anaheim Duck Olen Zellweger has signed a new deal with Buffalo after his trade to Western N.Y. last week on draft day. The 22 year old with three NHL seasons under his belt was a resticted free agent upon arrival. Zellweger’s deal is for three years and about 9 million dollars. (exact numbers to come) He is the top candadate as of now to take Bo Byram’s spot alongside Owen Power.




Olen Zellweger extends in BUF3 years, around $9M for him…nice for both team and player— Elliotte Friedman (@FriedgeHNIC) July 1, 2026




In 2022-23, Zellweger was voted the top defender among players in the OHL, QMJHL and WHL combined. He won the Canadian Hockey League Defenseman of the Year. The speedy Olen scored 22 points in 76 games last year for the Ducks and some are now calling him “Byram Lite”. He has a ways to go to fill Bo’s skates but he has all the tools. This feels like a fair deal for both sides as the Sabres figure out what they have with this player.

stories.title: Sabres ink Malenstyn to new long-term deal
stories.summary:
Malengoat stays! Free agency week started with the Buffalo Sabres locking up a key piece of the bottom six to a new six-year deal with Beck Malenstyn signing a new contract for $17.5 million, which works out to an AAV of $2.9167M. 



As a key piece of the Sabres fourth line, the fast-skating hard-hitting forward featured in 81 regular season games, scoring 7 goals and 7 assists averaging 11:15 a night with 38 PIM and a 40% faceoff win percentage. He added to that with 1 goal and 2 assists in 13 playoff games playing 10:30 a game. 



The new deal marks more than double his previous contract at 2x $1.35M, but it’s a well-earned raise given how essential he has been to the Sabres forward corps. 




MALENGOAT 🐐We have signed forward Beck Malenstyn to a six-year contract with an AAV of $2.9167 million.Details → https://t.co/REPC5llWvF pic.twitter.com/8B3rbt02GB— Buffalo Sabres (@BuffaloSabres) June 29, 2026




With Malenstyn now locked up, an integral part of the fourth line is in place along with Sam Carrick and the Sabres can focus on the top nine.

stories.title: Buffalo Sabres Open Thread, Wk of 6/29
stories.summary:
The NHL Draft has come and gone and the Buffalo Sabres have certainly been busy retooling the roster given there were some expiring contracts and players entering pivotal final seasons of their current deals. 



This week will see the opening of NHL Free Agency, starting at 12:00 p.m. ET on Wednesday July 1. Under the terms of the Collective Bargaining Agreement, any contact or contract negotiation between NHL teams and pending unrestricted free agents (UFAs) is strictly prohibited prior to the official opening, even though every year we see prior discussions all the time.



Between contract extensions, early trades, NMCs and NTCs, this has led to a pretty weak free agent market, but that should not dissuade aggressive General Managers from making moves. We’ll have a tracker going here on DBTB to update the latest signings on Wednesday.

stories.title: Day Two Draft Recap For the Sabres
stories.summary:
Buffalo was significantly quieter on day two of the draft. Due to their various trades, they only picked three times in rounds 2-7. There were over 100 selections between their picks as they selected Morozov at 20th overall and their next pick was at 124th overall. Whether it was intentional or just how the board fell, Buffalo added forward prospects with all three of their selections.



Round 4 Pick 124 – Olivers Murnieks – Center – 6’1” – (7-13-20 in 31 games in QMJHL)



Murnieks had a disappointing season offensively due to injuries but is an intriguing prospect. He is a high energy player who projects to be a bottom six forward in the NHL. His defensive reliability and motor set him apart from his peers. The Latvian native is committed to Boston College and plans to join the squad in the Fall. He’s young for his draft class as he turns 18 at the end of July. Between major junior and college, he’ll have plenty of opportunities to grow into a quality pro player.



Round 5 Pick 156 – Doman Szongoth – Center – 6’1” – (10-18-28 in 44 games in U20 SM-sarja)



Buffalo continued their run on centerman by selecting Szongoth in the 5th round. He is just the fifth Hungarian drafted by an NHL team. If he were to make the Sabres roster in the future, he would be the first of his countrymen to make their NHL debut. He is a talented two-way center that will need time to grow into his game. He is expected to return to the top junior league in Finland but could jump to the men’s league if he takes an additional step in development.



Round 6 Pick 188 – Dylan Dumont – Right Wing – 6’0” – (28-16-44 in 62 games in QMJHL)



Dumont is a scoring winger who attacks the center of the ice. He’s another young player for his draft (mid-August birthday) who hit his growth spirts in the last couple of years with room to continue to develop physically. He was his team’s leading goal scorer last year and is expected to be leaned on for goal production again next season. His style of play makes him one to keep an eye on as he rounds out his game.



Despite the lack of draft capital, Buffalo was able to add to their already impressive stockpile of young talent. Time will tell whether these prospects can make any noise at the NHL level. Fans will get their first look at them at prospect camp in the next few days at Harborcenter.

stories.title: The View from Patriots Wire
stories.summary:
Came across this article:
	
	“The Patriots have seemingly pulled away in the race with the many moves they've made in the offseason.



	 



	Meanwhile, the Bills haven't done much of anything to signal they'll be any better than they were last year. One could even argue that they'll take a step back after firing head coach Sean McDermott. Josh Allen is Superman on the football field, but he's going to need more than D.J. Moore and a middle-of-the-road defense to fly high enough to reach his first Super Bowl….



	 



	The Bills have to be seeing the moves being made by the Patriots, and it would be hard to imagine them feeling good about their offseason in relation to the competition. They are now faced with a situation where the Patriots have one of the best young quarterbacks in the league in Drake Maye and a top-10 defensive unit that gave Allen problems last season.



	 



	Will the Bills ever wake up and realize fighting fire with fire is much better than laying the hopes and dreams of an entire franchise on the shoulders of one man?”



	 



	Do you think this is justified or comically arrogant?
